coredata.dc:title: Multi-core@shell double confinement of carbon dots for giant enhancement of thermal Stability: Photoluminescence and lasing up to 1000 K

coredata.dc:description: Low thermal stability has been a common challenge for fluorescent nano-materials in high-power optoelectronic devices or high-temperature environment. In this paper, a double-confined strategy, which is conducted to giant enhancement of thermal stability, is designed for carbon dots (CDs) to form CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure. This structure can not only prevent CDs from thermal quenching of photoluminescence (PL) up to 1000 K, but also even achieve lasing at such a high temperature without gas protection, indicating the excellent protection of SiO2 multi-core@shell structure to resist the thermal quenching on one hand, while on the other hand enhance the PL and meanwhile stabilize the luminescence center of CDs. Hence, the CDs@SiO2 could find a potential application in high-temperature speckle-free laser imaging by using CDs random laser as light source. This research is a major step toward the application of fluorescence materials at high temperature, and meanwhile provides the novel compass to construction of thermally stable fluorescence materials and even laser gain mediums, which could be expected to make a great impact in the area of thermal damage monitoring, manufacturing and aerospace etc.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of synthesis of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure. (b) Comparison of working temperature for luminescent nano-materials.

(a) SEM image of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure with surface and cross section (b) TEM image of CDs@SiO2, the upper left inset is the HRTEM image of CDs, the lower left inset shows size distribution histogram of CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of CDs@SiO2 pyrolyzed at different temperatures. (d) XRD pattern of CDs@SiO2 pyrolyzed at 900 K.
(a) XPS spectra of CDs@SiO2. High-resolution XPS spectra corresponding to Si2p, C1s and O1s respectively of CDs@SiO2 pyrolyzed at (b) 500 K, (c) 700 K, (d) 800 K, (e) 900 K and (f) 1000 K.

(a) Excitation spectrum and PL spectra (collected at 800 K) of CDs@SiO2 fabricated with different content of micro-SiO2. (365 nm laser excitation) (b) PL spectra of CDs@SiO2 collected at different temperatures. (355 nm laser excitation) (c) Relative intensity (I0 is served as the original intensity at room temperature) of CDs@SiO2 versus working temperatures (black line) and working time (blue line), the insets are photographs of CDs@SiO2 under different working temperatures. (d) TGA (black line) and DSC (blue line) curves of CDs@SiO2.

Lasing spectra of cds@sio2 collected at (a) 400 K, (b) 600 K, (c) 800 K, (d) 900 K and (e) 1000 K, respectively, insets are the output-input curves of lasing emission collected at corresponding temperatures. (f) Variation of lasing threshold versus different temperatures, the inset on the upper left-hand corner is the schematic of random laser exciation and detection, and the inset on the bottom right-hand corner is the schematic presentation of lasing in CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure. (All the spectra above were obtained by excitation of 365 nm laser).

(a) schematic of speckle-free laser imaging under high temperature. photographs of CDs@SiO2 illuminated by (b) LED, (c) monochrome laser and (d) random laser, respectively. photographs of ZnO film illuminated by (e) LED, (f) monochrome laser and (g) random laser, respectively. photographs of TiO2 film illuminated by (h) LED, (i) monochrome laser and (j) random laser, respectively.

head.ce:title: Multi-core@shell double confinement of carbon dots for giant enhancement of thermal Stability: Photoluminescence and lasing up to 1000 K

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low thermal stability has been a common challenge for fluorescent nano-materials in high-power optoelectronic devices or high-temperature environment. In this paper, a double-confined strategy, which is conducted to giant enhancement of thermal stability, is designed for carbon dots (CDs) to form CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure. This structure can not only prevent CDs from thermal quenching of photoluminescence (PL) up to 1000 K, but also even achieve lasing at such a high temperature without gas protection, indicating the excellent protection of SiO2 multi-core@shell structure to resist the thermal quenching on one hand, while on the other hand enhance the PL and meanwhile stabilize the luminescence center of CDs. Hence, the CDs@SiO2 could find a potential application in high-temperature speckle-free laser imaging by using CDs random laser as light source. This research is a major step toward the application of fluorescence materials at high temperature, and meanwhile provides the novel compass to construction of thermally stable fluorescence materials and even laser gain mediums, which could be expected to make a great impact in the area of thermal damage monitoring, manufacturing and aerospace etc.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence nano-materials are the key components in varieties of fluorescence systems [1]. However, low fluorescence stability at high temperature, which is a common problem for all the fluorescence nano-materials, hinders their application in extreme environment [2]. Temperature control system is always needed for high temperature working. This makes the fluorescence system rather complicated. Quenching of fluorescence at high temperature is mainly resulted from destruction of molecules or crystal structures of fluorescence nano-materials. This is because nano sized molecules or crystals are particularly fragile and sensitive to thermal shock [3]. Besides, aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which is caused by high chaotic molecules motion at high temperature is another reason of fluorescence quenching [4]. As a consequence, a designed structure, which provides both protection and restriction of motion to the nano sized structure, is key to high fluorescence thermal stability.

To date, CDs have attracted tremendous attention owing to their low toxicity [5], low cost [6] and interesting optical properties [7], which have been widely used in miniaturized solid-state lasers [8,9], light-emitting diodes (LED) [10], anti-counterfeiting [11], and imaging of latent fingerprints [12]. However, like majority of other fluorescence materials, fluorescence of CDs is significantly suffered from low thermal stability. For the past few years, researchers have paid great efforts to improve their thermal stability through diversified strategies. Qu et al. fabricated the CDs encapsulated by BaSO4 to achieve PL emission at 573 K [13]. Yang et al. dispersed the CDs into the nanochannels of nanoporous glass to preserve fluorescence after treatment at 673 K [14]. Wu et al. reported in situ creation of CDs in confined silica compartments and observed fluorescence at 673 K [4]. Till now, the highest temperature of fluorescence is reported by Chang et al., who assembled nitrogen-doped CDs inside the mesoporous channels of SBA-15, realizing the fluorescence at 773 K [15]. However, the fluorescence intensity at 773 K remained only ～30 % of that at room temperature. Although, the fluorescence temperature is rather high, the fluorescence stability at high temperature is still low. Hence, novel type of CDs-based material working at higher temperature with significant higher retention of PL intensity should be further explored.

Here, we report the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure fabricated through a simple one-step pyrolysis process. The CDs are double-confined by micro-SiO2 core and SiO2 shell with linkage of Si-O-Si bonds respectively. This double confinement prevents the CDs from high temperature destruction on one hand and simultaneously amplify and stabilize the luminescence on the other hand. Fluorescent thermal stability of CDs at high temperature is remarkably enhanced by this strategy. The SiO2 shell plays an essential role to prevent the CDs from external thermal shock, protecting the luminescence structure of CDs. And the micro-SiO2 core significantly enhance the PL intensity through electromagnetic resonance and effectively prevent the aggregation of CDs to resist the ACQ effect. As a result, no thermal quenching of PL intensity from the CDs@SiO2 is observed up to 1000 K and lasing is also achieved at such an high temperature. To the best of our knowledge, this is the highest photoluminescence and lasing temperature record achieved till now (Fig. 1 b). The remarkable performance permits the speckle-free laser imaging at high temperature by using CDs@SiO2 as laser gain medium, which is expected to be applied in the real-time monitoring of micron grade deformation of workpieces or materials with raising temperature.

The CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure were synthesized through a simple one-step pyrolysis process. 3-(2-amino-ethylamine) propyl dimethoxy silane (A-2120), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and micro-SiO2 were selected as raw materials for preparation of the CDs@SiO2, while A-2120 is served as the carbon source for formation of CDs. In the fabrication process, pretreatment at 310 K is carried out for hydrolysis of TEOS and A-2120, in order to form the silicon dioxide network, which becomes the SiO2 shell eventually (Fig. 1a). It is noted that, skipping the pretreatment stage would lead to the significant evaporation of TEOS after direct pyrolysis, hindering the formation of SiO2 shell. After that, the mixture is pyrolyzed at 900 K for 40 min. During increasing the pyrolysis temperature, the micro-SiO2 is first modified by A-2120 which then forms the molecular fluorophore directly on the surface of micro-SiO2 at around 500 K [16]. With constantly increasing the temperature up to 900 K, the carbon core ultimately forms and becomes double-confined between the micro-SiO2 core and SiO2 shell with linkage of Si-O-Si bond. Giant enhancement of thermal stability of CDs for photoluminescence and lasing at 1000 K without gas protection, which is the highest value reported for CDs, is observed by the designed multi-core@shell double confinement structure. (Fig. 1a).

Mophorlogy of the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure is shown in the SEM images in Fig. 2 a. It is observed that micro-SiO2 particles are embedded in the SiO2 matrix to form a multi-core@shell structure. CDs are not observed in the SEM images due to insufficient resolution of SEM. Morphology of CDs is proved in the TEM image as shown in Fig. 2b. Given the confinement effect of SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, no obvious aggregation of CDs is observed. The CDs are nearly spherical with an average size of 3 nm (the inset of bottom left corner in Fig. 2b). The high-resolution TEM image in the inset of upper left corner in Fig. 2b shows a lattice fringe of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite.

To reveal the structure of CDs@SiO2, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure with different pyrolysis temperatures are compared in Fig. 2c. In the spectrum of the CDs@SiO2 at pyrolysis temperature of 500 K, the peaks located at 1657 cm−1 and 1258 cm−1 are attributed to the C = O stretch vibration [32] and Si-CH3 deformation vibration [33], which may derive from A-2120. The peaks centering at 3350 cm−1, 2925 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1, which stem from –OH/N-H, –CH2 and C = C, respectively, are observed. The FT-IR spectrum also demonstrates the presence of abundant Si-O-Si and C-Si at 1020 cm−1 and 765 cm−1. This implies that carbon-based fluorophores are formed at this stage. However, when the pyrolysis temperature reaches 900 K (the fabrication temperature), content of –OH, C = C, C = O and Si-CH3 decrease significantly, while, the peaks of CH2 and NH2 completely disappear in the spectra. This suggests that majority of organic groups on carbon-based fluorophores, which are sensitive to high temperature, are erased by high temperature. On contrary, CDs with graphite core and Si atoms (C-Si) doped on their surface, which are durable to high temperature, are formed by remaining carbon-based fluorophores. The functional groups (Si-O-C, Si-C) on the surface of CDs can make CDs linked to the SiO2 matrix with Si-O-Si bonds, which effectively confine the CDs to resist the aggregation on one hand, while on the other hand significantly enhance the intensity of CDs through robust covalent interactions [34]. Moreover, Si-O-Si linkages between CDs, micro-SiO2 and SiO2 shell are formed by dehydration of Si-OH, which are provided by hydrolysis of A-2120, TEOS and surface of micro-SiO2. The compact SiO2 shell with the linkage of Si-O-Si prevent doping of oxygen, which is the key to protect CDs at high temperature, in the multi-core@shell structure. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure are shown in Fig. 2d and Fig. S1. Numbers of sharp peaks are found on both of CDs@SiO2 and micro-SiO2 with different pyrolysis temperatures, indicating the crystal face of (100), (101), (110), (112), (211) and etc. Positions of these peaks are almost the same in different patterns, matching well with the reported quartz [35] and JCPDS #75–0443 (Fig. S1a). Absence of XRD signal of CDs is probably attributed to encapsulation of CDs by SiO2 shell.

To further investigate the composition of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses are also performed. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, five peaks around 532 eV, 399 eV, 285 eV, 154 eV and 103 eV, which are attributed to O1s, N1s, C1s, Si2s and Si2p respectively, are observed in the wide-scan XPS spectrum of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure pyrolyzed at 500 K. It is obvious that the relative peak intensity of C1s becomes weaker gradually when increasing the pyrolysis temperature, while the peak of N1s totally absent over the pyrolysis temperature of 800 K. The atomic proportion of carbon varies from 45.51 % at pyrolysis temperature of 500 K to 7.17 % at pyrolysis temperature of 900 K (the fabrication temperature of our sample), and shows no significant change when pyrolysis temperature surpasses 900 K (Table S1). The variation of atomic proportions indicates that the N atom and the partial of C atom from A-2120 are oxidized into nitrogen and carbon-based volatile substances respectively when the pyrolysis temperature increases before 900 K. Subsequently, the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure reaches steady state after CDs formed at 900 K. Moreover, high-resolution spectra of Si2p, C1s, O1s are also depicted in Fig. 3b-f. As shown in Fig. 3b, the spectrum of Si2p can be fitted into two peaks at 102.39 eV and 103.52 eV, corresponding to Si-O and SiO2 respectively [36]. The spectrum of C1s exhibits five peaks at 284.00 eV, 284.61 eV, 285.31 eV, 286.63 eV and 288.53 eV, which are ascribed to C-Si, C = C, C-C, C-O and C = O groups. Three peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.36 eV and 533.09 eV are also observed in the spectrum of O1s which are related to the presence of C-O, Si-O and SiO2 respectively. Obviously, as increase of the pyrolysis temperature, the peaks of Si-O (derived from raw materials as A-2120 and TEOS) in spectra of Si2p and O1s lower down, and almost disappear eventually, while the peaks attributed to SiO2 become dominated in both of two spectra. Therefore, the XPS results indicate that the raw materials (A-2120 and TEOS) decompose on one hand, while on the other hand the SiO2 multi-core@shell structure is formed with increasing the pyrolysis temperature, which show consistency with the FTIR analysis results.

To further illustrate the construction proposed above from the morphology aspect, optical micrographs with different pyrolysis temperatures are shown in Fig. S2. During increasing the pyrolysis temperature from 500 K to 700 K, the color of the CDs@SiO2 darkens, indicating that the oxidation of A-2120. When the pyrolysis temperature exceeds 800 K, the color becomes lighter, which is attributed to the complete decomposition of A-2120. The redundant C atoms on carbon-based fluorophores which are not transformed into CDs oxidize into carbon based volatile substances eventually. In the meantime, the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure forms and randomly arranges as a film exhibited in Fig. S2d, showing consistency with the results of characterization discussed above. Hence, the core–shell double confinement of CDs is the key for giant enhancement of thermal stability.

To confirm the operating excitation wavelength, excitation spectrum of CDs@SiO2 was investigated in Fig. 4 a. The obtained CDs show an optimal excitation peak at around 365 nm corresponding to the emission at 450 nm. Hence, 365 nm (355 nm) excitation source was used in the following research.

As what we mentioned above, micro-SiO2 plays an important role in the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure. To figure out the optimal content of micro-SiO2, spectra of CDs@SiO2 with different content of micro-SiO2 are illustrated in Figs. S3a and 4a. It is obvious that the CDs@SiO2 shows a maximum intensity when content of micro-SiO2 is set to be 30 wt% under both 300 K and 800 K (Fig. S3b and c). This is because excess of micro-SiO2 may increase the excitation light loss, while insufficiency of micro-SiO2 would be not able to provide enough binding sites to CDs. In addition, reaction conditions including preparation temperature and time are also investigated. As exhibited in Fig. S3d, e and Fig. S4, the maximum emission can be obtained at pyrolysis temperature of 900 K for 40 min. Based on these results, the optimal reaction conditions are confirmed.

Fig. S5a shows ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of CDs@SiO2 under different pyrolysis temperatures. The sharp peak at about 245 nm can be assigned to the π-π* transition of C = C [37], while the broad tail band in the range from 300 nm to 500 nm, which entirely overlaps with the excitation spectrum, is ascribed to the n-π* edge transition [38]. It can be also observed that the absorption intensity becomes higher with increasing the pyrolysis temperature before 800 K. And the decline of intensity occurs when the pyrolysis temperature exceeds 800 K. It is because the oxidation of the alkane deepens when rising the pyrolysis temperature before 800 K, which results in the enhancement of the absorption. In this stage, the absorption is attributed to both the CDs and carbon-based fluorophores. As discussed above, with further increasing the pyrolysis temperature, other carbonized part which were not transformed into CDs is oxidized into carbon based volatile substances. Hence, the absorption is mainly contributed by CDs when the temperature exceeds 800 K. Especially for 1000 K, it is a very high temperature, which partially destroyed the chromophore moieties on CDs, leading to the decline of the absorption peak. The emission spectra were detected at various wavelengths ranging from 355 nm to 415 nm (Fig. S5b), showing a strong excitation dependence. This is a common encountered feature of CDs [39].

To investigate the heat-resistance performance at high temperature, photoluminescence spectra of CDs@SiO2 obtained under different operating temperatures are illustrated in Fig. 4b. Unexpectedly, no thermal quenching of PL intensity from the CDs@SiO2 is observed with rising operating temperature from room temperature to 1000 K without additional gas protection (Fig. 4c). To the best of our knowledge, both of the retention rate and ultimate working temperature are the highest value achieved. Almost no obvious difference on the PL intensity is witnessed by naked eye at working temperatures range from 300 K to 1000 K as shown in the inset of Fig. 4c.

Thermal stability under long-term heating, which is also explored at 800 K as illustrated in Fig. 4c, is quite important for practical use. The PL intensity remains unchanged at 1000 K compared with that at room temperature and retains 92 % of the original value after heating at 800 K for 4 h. Shapes of PL spectra of CDs@SiO2 remain unchanged after this thermal treatment (Fig. S5c). In addition, Fig. 4d shows the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curve of CDs@SiO2, in which the weight loss mainly occurs before 100 °C and becomes slight after 100 °C. It can be attributed to the evaporation of water and other evolved gas molecules adhering to the surface of CDs@SiO2 in the process of increasing the temperature before 100 °C [40]. The results of TGA are further confirmed by differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) analysis in Fig. 4d, since an obvious endothermic peak appears at 100 °C or so, which also indicates the evaporization of water and other evolved gas molecules. The results of TGA and DSC jointly confirm the resistance of oxidation of CDs at high temperature with slight change of weight loss.

In order to investigate the origin of thermal stability of the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, it is necessary to figure out the roles and functions of each part of the SiO2 multi-core@shell structure. Started with the SiO2 shell, the tight SiO2 network encapsulate the CDs with lingkage of Si-O-Si bond, which effectively protect the CDs from external thermal quenching (Fig. 5 ). Studies by Louis et al. demonstrated that the initial oxidation is likely to occur on the outermost layer of the CDs at high temperature [41], while the Si-O-Si bonds on the surface of CDs as the linkage between CDs and SiO2 shell can effectively confine the carbon shell to resist the oxidation. It is the key to avoid the luminescence structure being destroyed. The SiO2 shell can efficiently block the environmental quenchers (such as water and oxygen), hampering oxidation and destruction of structure of CDs at high temperature [42]. The emission of CDs is thus stabilized under different temperatures. Fig. S6 shows long time treatment of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure to destroy TEOS-formed SiO2 shell. As shown in Fig. S6, after a long time treatment at 1000 K, the thermal stability of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure gets significantly worse. This is due to the destruction of the TEOS-formed SiO2 shell at such a high temperature, resulting in the loss of protection for CDs. It is obvious that the block-like features of the SiO2 shell (Fig. S2d) become indiscernible in the photograph of the CDs@SiO2 pyrolyzed at 1000 K (Fig. S2e). In addition, the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure without pretreatment also exhibits worse thermal stability above 800 K, which is ascribed to the incompleteness of formation of the SiO2 shell (Fig. S7). Although the silane A-2120 can also form the SiO2 shell through the hydrolysis of alkoxide, the compactness of this shell is not well as that formed by TEOS since the TEOS has two more functional alkoxy groups than A-2120 [43]. For comparison, other kinds of shell (GeO2 and TiO2) are also used to encapsulate the CDs (Fig. S8). The intensity of the CDs covered by GeO2 shell decreases constantly with increasing the operating temperature, while the CDs coated by TiO2 shell entirely show no PL emission under excitation of UV laser (the insets of Fig. S8b). This indicates that the TEOS-formed SiO2 shell is indispensable in this system.

As for the micro-SiO2 core, it is attached by CDs with linkage of Si-O-Si bond to resist the ACQ effect on one hand, while on the other hand it simultaneously enhance the PL intensity through the electromagnetic resonance [34]. As performed in Fig. S9a, the samples fabricated with micro-SiO2 can obviously show stronger emission than those without micro-SiO2 at 800 K. The strong electromagnetic field generated in the vicinity of dielectric SiO2 (dielectric constant: 3.9) can effectively enhance the PL intensity of coupled fluorescence emitters [44]. Fig. S9b exhibits the FTIR spectra of micro-SiO2, silane A-2120 and the composite of them annealed at 500 K. It is clearly witnessed that the characteristic peaks of –CH2 and Si-CH3, which derived from the silane, are observed on the spectrum of the composite. This confirms the successful decoration of the silane molecule on the surface of micro-SiO2. Moreover, the –OH group shown in the spectrum of the micro-SiO2 disappears in that of the composite, and the characteristic peak of Si-O-Si (C-O-Si) of micro-SiO2 (silane) also shifts to the right in the spectrum of the composite. All these results prove that the micro-SiO2 is firstly modified by the silane molecule when the pyrolysis temperature exceeds 500 K or so. And the linkage of Si-O-Si occurs in the reaction between the –OH of micro-SiO2 and the alkoxide of the silane. Referring to the common strategy to improve the thermal stability of luminescent materials [45–47], the CDs@SiO2 with only SiO2 shell confinement was fabricated for comparison (i. e.: CDs were merely embedded into the SiO2 shells without micro-SiO2), which shows a continuous decline on PL intensity with increasing the temperature (Fig. S9c, d). It can be observed that the PL intensity of the CDs/SiO2 composite without micro-SiO2 quenches by 49 % at 800 K, indicating a much worse thermal stability than the CDs@SiO2 with micro-SiO2. In other words, micro-SiO2 is also key to enhance thermal stability of CDs at high temperature. Moreover, the micro-SiO2 kept good structural integrity after being treated at 900 K to well provide the attachment points to CDs (insets of Fig. S9d). In addition, other kinds of core (GeO2 and TiO2) are also employed to compare, while the shell is still made by SiO2. As shown in Fig. S10a and b, the CDs with GeO2 core can keep above 90 % intensity of room temperature before the operating temperature exceeds 800 K, whereas a dramatic decline of intensity begins above 900 K. Besides, in terms of the CDs with TiO2 core, only emission from the TiO2 particles is observed under excitation of 355 nm laser. This is mainly attributed to the strong absorption of TiO2 particles at around 415 nm. The emission of CDs at approximate 430 nm is thus inner filtered by TiO2 particles, while the weak emission at 580 nm is able to be witnessed under excitation of 420 nm laser (Fig. S10c).

It is worthy to mention that, the Si-O-Si bond between the CDs and the core, the shell respectively is also crucial in this structure. This covalent bond is able to make a difference to the energy level of the CDs, as evidenced by the redshift of the emission of CDs when the core or the shell is replaced by GeO2, while the absorption remains unchanged (Fig. S11a). Moreover, we compared both the absorption and the emission of CDs-2 and CDs-2@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, which are fabricated by using citric acid as raw materials instead of A-2120 without Si doping. No obvious difference is observed in Fig. S11b due to the lack of Si-O-Si bond on the surface of CDs-2. We then successfully seperated the free CDs from the SiO2 matrix in ethanol solution. A-2120 was served as single raw material that derectly pyrolyzed at 900 K for 40 min. The product was then ground and dispersed in the ethanol solution under ultrasound. The free CDs can be obtained through filting the supernatant after the ethanol solution was sufficiently stood. Morphology of free CDs is exhibited in Fig. S12a. The size of free CDs is in the range of 1.25–2.5 nm, finding a peak at 1.75 nm (inset of Fig. S12a). It is noted that, the smaller sizes compared to CDs confined in SiO2 multi-core@shell structure can be attributed to the lack of double confinement, which leads to reduction of the surface groups during the calcination process [48]. As shown in Fig. S12b, free CDs show a narrower spectrum in comparison of CDs@SiO2 due to lack of Si-O-Si bonds linking to the SiO2 matrix. The Si-O-Si bonds are able to enlarge and stabilize the energy level of CDs. Nevertheless, the emission peak of the CDs remains at 430 nm after linking to the SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, unlike those replaced by other kinds of core or shell, which implies that the stability of the energy level of the CDs in pure SiO2 matrix is better than that in other matrix. Hence, the match with the SiO2 multi-core@shell structure helps the Si-O-Si bond competent to stabilize the energy level of CDs even at high temperature, to resist the thermal quenching. To further demonstrate the importance of the SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, comparison of thermal stability with free CDs is therefore investigated. As performed in Fig. s12c and d, the free CDs without any protection show continuous decline of emission intensity during increasing the temperature, indicating the worse thermal stability than the CDs confined in the SiO2 multi-core@shell structure. In general, four main mechanisms can be proposed to explain the PL behaviors of CDs, including core state emission, surface state emission, molecular state emission, and crosslink-enhanced emissions [49]. In consideration of a fact that most of organic functional groups on the surface of CDs are destroyed during the calcination process (Fig. 2c), oxygen-rich surface state emission and intrinsic emission from the carbongenic core of CDs are regarded as the origin of PL in the CDs@SiO2, as shown in Fig. S12e [50].

To sum up, the excellent thermal stability of CDs was attributed to not only the double confinement of both the SiO2 core and the shell, but also to the essential role of the Si-O-Si bond. In comparison with other types of matrices, the SiO2 matrix significantly enhances the thermal stability of CDs to the greatest extent, demonstrating its distinct role in this structure.

What’s more, this CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure is a universal stratagy to enhance thermal stability of CDs. As reported in previous publication, the CDs confined in the silica matrices formed by TEOS maintain outstanding thermal stability at 673 K. Nevertheless, the PL intensity dramatically decreases at 773 K and even vanished at 873 K [4]. CDs with similar structure by using the same raw materials as that in the pubilication were fabricated (as shown in SI: Synthesis of CDs-1). In comparison, the CDs-1 are double confined in a SiO2 multi-core@shell structure. As shown in Fig. S13, the photoluminescence of CDs-1@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure maintains over 22 % of original intensity at 1000 K. It is because one layer of confinement by simple SiO2 shell is insufficient to provide protection to CDs when temperature exceeds 673 K, while double confinement of SiO2 multi-core@shell structure can effectively isolate the external oxidation and simultaneously stablize the luminescence center, with linkage of Si-O-Si bond, at higher temperatures. The inset of Fig. S13b exhibits the photoluminescence image of CDs-1@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure at 1000 K. In addition, CDs-2 and CDs-2@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, which were fabricated by using citric acid as carbon source without Si doping, are also compared. It can be observed in Fig. S14a that the CDs-2@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure shows PL emission at 700 K, whereas the PL intensity of unprotected CDs-2 almost vanishes at 500 K (Fig. S14b). The CDs-2@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure exhibits significantly higher PL retention than that of unprotected CDs-2 (Fig. S14c). This indicates that the SiO2 multi-core@shell structure can still protect the CDs without formation of Si-O-Si linkage to some extent and Si-O-Si linkage is indispensable to achieve even higher thermal stability.

To sum up, the high thermal stability of CDs is attributed to the following three aspects: (1) majority of organic groups on carbon-based fluorophores are erased by high fabrication temperature. The remained highly graphitized CDs, whose structure is similar to graphite, is highly thermal stable in an environment without oxygen. (2) SiO2 shell, which is linked with CDs by Si-O-Si linkage is the key to prevent CDs oxidation at high temperature in the multi-core@shell structure. In addition, studies by Louis et al. demonstrated that the initial oxidation is likely to occur on the outermost layer of the CDs at high temperature, while the Si-O-Si bonds on the surface of CDs as the linkage between CDs and the SiO2 multi-core@shell structure can effectively confine the carbon shell to resist the oxidation. (3) the micro-SiO2 core, which is attached by CDs with linkage of Si-O-Si bonds, resist the ACQ effect on one hand, while on the other hand it simultaneously enhances the PL intensity through the electromagnetic resonance. In consideration of a fact that most of organic functional groups on the surface of CDs are destroyed during the calcination process, oxygen-rich surface state emission and intrinsic emission from the carbongenic core of CDs are regarded as the origin of PL in the CDs@SiO2. As a result, it is possible to prepared longer wavelength emission thermal stable CDs by doping an amount of heteroatoms such as N in carbongenic core of CDs to reduce band gap of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High temperature lasing up to 1000 K
ce:section.ce:para: Given the increasing interest in the field of CDs, CDs-based lasers are nowadays garnering significant attention [8,51,52]. Bestowed with the excellent thermal stability of CDs@SiO2, lasing is able to be realized at high temperature. As shown in the inset on the upper left-hand corner of Fig. 6 f, the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell film was directly excited by a 365 nm nanosecond pulsed laser beam to achieve population inversion. In this system, the micro-SiO2 core can efficiently increase the optical scattering as a type of scattering media. The shell can play a role of optical resonator to extend the feedback path. Both of the core and the shell jointly enhance the optical gain to promote the stimulated emission (bottom right-hand corner in Fig. 6f). As shown in Figs. 6a–e and S15a–c, a few sharp peaks are observed (see line ‘2′ of these figures) when the excitation power exceeds the threshold value (knots in the input–output curves shown in the insets of these figures). With further increasing the excitation power, more lasing modes are emerged from the spectra, covering entire gain bandwidth. However, distribution of the lasing modes is not uniform over the emission spectra. This is due to the random scattering of light introduced by the unevenly distributed micro-SiO2. The signal to noise ratio (SNR) of spectra in Fig. 6e seems lower than other lasing spectra despite the PL intensity of CDs remains unchanged at 1000 K. This is mainly due to the infrared radiation from heating equipment. When the temperature exceeds 800 K, infrared radiation can be generated from the metal plate which is used to heat our samples. Especially above 1000 K, thermal radiation of metal blue shift and enhance, part of red light can be thus collected by the spectrometer (the inset of Fig. 4c). Therefore, noise was induced in the lasing spectra in Fig. 6e within the range above 650 nm.

Fig. 6f shows the laser threshold at different temperatures. It is noted that, the threshold slightly increases with the temperature rising before 800 K, and then illustrates a noticeable elevation when further elevate the operating temperature. It is known that one of the conditions to achieve lasing emission is the optical gain exceeding optical loss. With increasing the temperature, lattice vibration become significant and increase the probability of exciton–phonon elastic scattering processing, which increase the optical loss [53]. In spite of the PL intensities of CDs keep stable, the increasing optical loss from cavity also leads to the increase of lasing threshold. However, lasing emission of our CDs@SiO2 is able to be achieved at even 1000 K, which is the highest value for CDs laser, demonstrating that the optical gain is still high enough to support lasing at such a high temperature. In addition, in the CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, scattering and interference of light inside the disordered gain media play an important role in random lasing. Scattering light transport length is the main factor for total amount of gain in the cavity. Enlargement of excitation area (Ath) is conducive to extend the path length traveled through the amplifying medium, which can enhance the optical gain and thus reduce the lasing threshold (Pth). For random lasers, Ath and Pth are expected to match the prediction of random lasing theory, where the Ath 2/3 should be proportional to 1/Pth [54]. Hence, we plotted the Ath 2/3 versus 1/Pth for CDs@SiO2 treated at 400 K, 600 K and 800 K, respectively, as shown in Fig. S15d–f. As envisioned, linear relationship between Ath 2/3 and 1/Pth is exhibited in these figures, with consistency to the random lasing theory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Speckle-free laser self-imaging under high temperature
ce:section.ce:para: As an important application of CDs, microcavity laser have been extensively studied [55–57], since we first reported the lasing emission from CDs in organic solvents [58]. Especially for random laser, the low spatial coherence without spoiling the lasing brightness can significantly ease the speckle problems in imaging, which are arisen by the interference derived from the high spatial coherence of typical monochrome laser [59]. The higher photon density, which can well improve the resolution of images comparing to the common incoherent light source such as light emitting diodes and arc lamp, also make the random laser more suitable to be applied in speckle-free laser imaging as the light source.

Based on the results shown above, the CDs@SiO2 with double confinement were proved to have perfect emission retention and stability at 800 K, therefore holding the great promise as powerful low-coherence light source for imaging in high-temperature environment, while other solid-state CDs were easily damaged at such a high temperature. As shown in the inset on the bottom right-hand corner of Fig. S5c, CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure exhibits high thermal stability under long-term operation at 800 K, while the time stability reduces when temperature become higher than 800 K due to slow evaporation of SiO2 under such high temperature [60]. For practical use, we choose 800 K as the best operation temperature. Hence, we proposed a speckle-free laser self-imaging strategy based on CDs@SiO2, which can be utilized upon high temperature (working temperature at 800 K). Fig. 7 a illustrates the configuration for speckle-free laser self-imaging. For imaging area, reflecting mirror (aluminum mirror) is placed on the heating platform, with a quartz plate coated with the CDs@SiO2 putting on top of it. Meantime, a blank quartz plate with thickness of 2 mm is also sandwiched between the mirror and the quartz plate coated with the sample. The detecting section is comprised of the combinatorial objective lens and a CMOS detector to collect the images. In this system, the sample is excited directly by the focused laser beam (365 nm pulse laser). The lasing emission derived from the CDs@SiO2 is then reflected by the mirror back to illuminate the sample. In other words, the CDs@SiO2 provides random laser as a light source, while it is also illuminated as the imaged object simultaneously. It is noted that, the function of the blank quartz plate is to extend the optical paths. This is because the random lasing beam derived from the CDs@SiO2 was divergent. Appropriate distance of optical paths is beneficial to enlarge the illumination area with ensuring the illumination intensity. Subsequently, the images are collected by the detecting section, which are then displayed on a computer. For comparison, other light sources are also used for imaging as shown in Fig. 7b and c. Started with light-emitting diode (LED), big cracks can be witnessed while the information of some small seams are lost (Fig. 7b). For conventional single-wavelength laser illumination (450 nm pulse laser), although the resolution is improved due to the high photon density, the imaging quality is still poor because of the interference (i. e. laser speckle) originated from monochrome laser [59]. Nevertheless, sharp images are observed from Fig. 7d without laser speckle under random lasing illumination. These results indicate that lasing emission with low spatial coherence can be obtained from our CDs@SiO2 even under high working temperature, which finds applications in high-temperature imaging system.

This high temperature speckle-free laser imaging strategy is also demonstrated to be a versatile imaging platform for different kinds of materials. As shown the demonstration in the dotted area in Fig. 7a, the CDs@SiO2 which is ground into powder was deposited on the corner of the quartz plate, while the remaining area was coated by the film fabricated with other materials (ZnO and TiO2). Random laser can be obtained by exciting the CDs@SiO2, and then images the film as light source with low spatial coherence. Therefore, as illuminated in Fig. 7g ang j, speckle-free laser images are collected with higher resolution compared to those with illumination by LEDs and single wavelength laser (Fig. 7e, f, h, i). As shown in Fig. S5c, good thermal stability under long-term heating can be observed from CDs@SiO2. For optical cavity, heating time would not change the optical gain in general, which helps lasing emission keep stable. Thus, continuous high-temperature operation would not affect the imaging quality.

Here, we designed a novel construction of CDs@SiO2 multi-core@shell structure, in which the CDs are double confined by the SiO2 shell together with the SiO2 core, with linkage of Si-O-Si bonds. It can not only effectively disperse the CDs at high temperature to resist ACQ and effectively enhance PL intensity of CDs, but also prevent the CDs from external thermal quenching and simultaneously stabilize the luminescence center. The CDs@SiO2 shows no thermal quenching of PL intensity up to 1000 K, which is the highest value achieved. They can also exhibit PL/laser at 1000 K without gas protection, the highest surviving temperature for CDs, indicating the excellent thermal stability. As a result, the random laser derived from CDs@SiO2 is verified to be an ideal light source with low coherence to produce speckle-free laser images at high temperature. This work will be the key component to overcome the low thermal stability issues of nano-materials, and leads a way for rational design of CDs, having a great importance for further application in high performance photoelectric devices.
